theory.intro: Every right-angled triangle has one side that stands out: the hypotenuse. Being able to spot it is the first step towards using Pythagoras' theorem.
facts: A **right-angled triangle** has one $90^\circ$ angle, shown in a diagram by a small square at the vertex.
The **hypotenuse** is the side **opposite** the right angle — it never touches the right angle.
The hypotenuse is always the **longest** side of a right-angled triangle.
The two shorter sides that meet at the right angle are the **legs** (the shorter sides).
The hypotenuse can point in any direction; its position depends only on where the right angle is, not on how the triangle is drawn.

foundation.question_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.8]\coordinate (A) at (0,0);\coordinate (B) at (4,0);\coordinate (C) at (0,3);\draw (A)--(B) node[midway,below] {$t$};\draw (A)--(C) node[midway,left] {$s$};\draw (B)--(C) node[midway,above right] {$r$};\coordinate (va) at ($(A)!0.3cm!(B)$);\coordinate (vb) at ($(A)!0.3cm!(C)$);\draw (va)--($(va)+(vb)-(A)$)--(vb);\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
Name the hypotenuse of this right-angled triangle.
foundation.solution_text: The hypotenuse is $r$, the side opposite the right angle.

foundation.question_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.8]\coordinate (A) at (4,0);\coordinate (B) at (0,0);\coordinate (C) at (4,3);\draw (A)--(B) node[midway,below] {$m$};\draw (A)--(C) node[midway,right] {$n$};\draw (B)--(C) node[midway,above left] {$p$};\coordinate (va) at ($(A)!0.3cm!(B)$);\coordinate (vb) at ($(A)!0.3cm!(C)$);\draw (va)--($(va)+(vb)-(A)$)--(vb);\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
Name the hypotenuse of this right-angled triangle.
foundation.solution_text: The hypotenuse is $p$, the side opposite the right angle.

foundation.question_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.8]\coordinate (A) at (0,3);\coordinate (B) at (0,0);\coordinate (C) at (4,3);\draw (A)--(B) node[midway,left] {$e$};\draw (A)--(C) node[midway,above] {$f$};\draw (B)--(C) node[midway,below right] {$d$};\coordinate (va) at ($(A)!0.3cm!(B)$);\coordinate (vb) at ($(A)!0.3cm!(C)$);\draw (va)--($(va)+(vb)-(A)$)--(vb);\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
Name the hypotenuse of this right-angled triangle.
foundation.solution_text: The hypotenuse is $d$, the side opposite the right angle.

foundation.question_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.8]\coordinate (J) at (0,2);\coordinate (L) at (-2,0);\coordinate (K) at (2,0);\draw (J)--(L);\draw (L)--(K);\draw (K)--(J);\node[above] at (J) {$J$};\node[below left] at (L) {$L$};\node[below right] at (K) {$K$};\coordinate (va) at ($(J)!0.3cm!(L)$);\coordinate (vb) at ($(J)!0.3cm!(K)$);\draw (va)--($(va)+(vb)-(J)$)--(vb);\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
The right angle is at $J$. Which side is the hypotenuse?
foundation.solution_text: Side $LK$, the side opposite the right angle at $J$.

development.question_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.8]\coordinate (J) at (1,1.732);\coordinate (L) at (-2,0);\coordinate (K) at (2,0);\draw (J)--(L) node[midway,above left] {$q$};\draw (J)--(K) node[midway,above right] {$r$};\draw (L)--(K) node[midway,below] {$p$};\coordinate (va) at ($(J)!0.3cm!(L)$);\coordinate (vb) at ($(J)!0.3cm!(K)$);\draw (va)--($(va)+(vb)-(J)$)--(vb);\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
The right angle is at the top vertex. Name the hypotenuse.
development.solution_text: The hypotenuse is $p$. Even though it is the horizontal base, it is the side opposite the right angle.

development.question_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.8]\coordinate (A) at (0,0);\coordinate (B) at (4,0);\coordinate (C) at (0,3);\draw (A)--(B) node[midway,below] {$8$};\draw (A)--(C) node[midway,left] {$6$};\draw (B)--(C) node[midway,above right] {$10$};\coordinate (va) at ($(A)!0.3cm!(B)$);\coordinate (vb) at ($(A)!0.3cm!(C)$);\draw (va)--($(va)+(vb)-(A)$)--(vb);\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
Which side length is the hypotenuse?
development.solution_text: $10$, the side opposite the right angle (also the longest side).

development.question_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.8]\coordinate (A) at (0,0);\coordinate (B) at (3,1);\coordinate (C) at (-1,3);\draw (A)--(B) node[midway,below right] {$f$};\draw (A)--(C) node[midway,left] {$g$};\draw (B)--(C) node[midway,above right] {$e$};\coordinate (va) at ($(A)!0.3cm!(B)$);\coordinate (vb) at ($(A)!0.3cm!(C)$);\draw (va)--($(va)+(vb)-(A)$)--(vb);\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
This right-angled triangle is drawn on a slant. Name the hypotenuse.
development.solution_text: The hypotenuse is $e$, the side opposite the right angle at $A$.

development.question_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.8]\coordinate (P) at (0,0);\coordinate (Q) at (3,0);\coordinate (R) at (3,2);\draw (P)--(Q);\draw (Q)--(R);\draw (R)--(P);\node[below left] at (P) {$P$};\node[below right] at (Q) {$Q$};\node[above right] at (R) {$R$};\coordinate (va) at ($(Q)!0.3cm!(P)$);\coordinate (vb) at ($(Q)!0.3cm!(R)$);\draw (va)--($(va)+(vb)-(Q)$)--(vb);\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
The right angle is at $Q$. Name the hypotenuse using the vertex letters.
development.solution_text: Side $PR$, the side opposite the right angle at $Q$.

mastery.question_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.8]\coordinate (A) at (0,0);\coordinate (B) at (2,-1);\coordinate (C) at (1,2);\draw (A)--(B) node[midway,below] {$u$};\draw (A)--(C) node[midway,left] {$v$};\draw (B)--(C) node[midway,right] {$w$};\coordinate (va) at ($(A)!0.3cm!(B)$);\coordinate (vb) at ($(A)!0.3cm!(C)$);\draw (va)--($(va)+(vb)-(A)$)--(vb);\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
The triangle is rotated so the right angle points down. Name the hypotenuse.
mastery.solution_text: The hypotenuse is $w$, the side opposite the right angle at $A$.

mastery.question_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.8]\coordinate (A) at (0,0);\coordinate (B) at (4,0);\coordinate (C) at (0,3);\draw (A)--(B) node[midway,below] {$12$};\draw (A)--(C) node[midway,left] {$9$};\draw (B)--(C) node[midway,above right] {$15$};\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
This triangle is right-angled, but the right angle is not marked. Which side is the hypotenuse?
mastery.solution_text: $15$. The hypotenuse is the longest side, and it lies opposite the right angle.

mastery.question_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.9]\coordinate (A) at (0,0);\coordinate (W) at (0,2.5);\coordinate (F) at (2,0);\draw (A)--(W) node[midway,left] {wall};\draw (A)--(F) node[midway,below] {ground};\draw (W)--(F) node[midway,above right] {ladder};\coordinate (va) at ($(A)!0.3cm!(F)$);\coordinate (vb) at ($(A)!0.3cm!(W)$);\draw (va)--($(va)+(vb)-(A)$)--(vb);\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
A ladder leans against a vertical wall, meeting the level ground at a right angle between the wall and the ground. Which part of the diagram is the hypotenuse?
mastery.solution_text: The ladder. It is the side opposite the right angle formed by the wall and the ground.
